socialHistory.smoking: Former smoker, quit 3 years ago (smoked ~5 years, half pack/day)
socialHistory.exercise: Physically demanding job. No formal exercise routine.
socialHistory.notes: Single, lives with roommate. Works 50+ hour weeks during building season. Construction foreman.

conditions.notes: Acute onset during work — lifting heavy materials at construction site. Pain radiating to left leg suggesting radiculopathy.
Left-sided radicular symptoms. MRI ordered to evaluate for disc herniation. Positive straight leg raise on left at 40 degrees.

code.display: SARS-COV-2 (COVID-19) vaccine, mRNA, spike protein, LNP, preservative free, 30 mcg/0.3mL dose, tris-sucrose formulation

encounters.reasonForVisit: Annual physical — new patient establishment. Required for union insurance.

notes.subjective: 34 y/o male presenting for new patient establishment and annual physical. Required for union insurance enrollment. No current complaints. Reports quitting smoking approximately 1 year ago after 5 years at half a pack per day. Physically active at work as a construction foreman. No regular medications. No known allergies. Father with T2DM diagnosed at 52.
notes.objective: Well-appearing male in no acute distress. HEENT: normocephalic, atraumatic, PERRLA, oropharynx clear. Neck: supple, no lymphadenopathy. Lungs: clear to auscultation bilaterally. Heart: RRR, no murmurs. Abdomen: soft, non-tender, non-distended. Musculoskeletal: full ROM all extremities. Skin: no concerning lesions. Neuro: alert, oriented x4, CN II-XII intact.
notes.assessment: Healthy 32-year-old male. Former smoker. Family history notable for paternal T2DM. BMI 27.1, slightly overweight. No acute issues identified.
notes.plan: 1. Routine labs: BMP ordered. 2. Immunizations updated: Tdap booster and COVID-19 bivalent administered today. 3. Counseled on continued tobacco abstinence. 4. Discussed weight management — encouraged structured exercise beyond occupational activity. 5. Return in 1 year or PRN.

encounters.reasonForVisit: Acute low back pain after work injury — lifting incident on construction site 2 days ago. Pain worsening, now radiating down left leg.

notes.subjective: 34 y/o male construction foreman presents with acute low back pain after lifting a steel beam 2 days ago at work. Reports immediate onset of sharp pain in the lower back. Over the past 48 hours pain has begun radiating down the left posterior thigh and into the calf. Rates pain 7/10 at rest, 9/10 with movement. Reports tingling in left foot intermittently. Denies bowel or bladder dysfunction. Denies saddle anesthesia. Denies fever. Unable to work since injury. Workers' compensation claim filed. No prior back injuries. Tried ibuprofen at home with minimal relief.
notes.objective: Male in mild distress, guarding lumbar spine. Gait antalgic, favoring left side. Lumbar spine: significant paraspinal muscle spasm bilaterally, worse on left. Tenderness to palpation at L4-L5 and L5-S1. Flexion limited to approximately 40 degrees by pain. Extension limited to 10 degrees. Straight leg raise positive on left at 40 degrees reproducing radicular pain. Straight leg raise negative on right. Motor: 5/5 bilateral lower extremities except left EHL 4+/5. Sensation: decreased to light touch along left L5 dermatome. DTRs: 2+ bilateral patellar and Achilles. No clonus. Perianal sensation intact.
notes.assessment: 1. Acute lumbar radiculopathy, left-sided, likely L4-L5 or L5-S1 disc herniation given mechanism of injury, positive SLR, subtle EHL weakness, and L5 dermatomal sensory changes. 2. Acute low back pain, work-related injury. Red flags (cauda equina) not present at this time. However, given motor findings (EHL weakness), advanced imaging warranted urgently.
notes.plan: 1. MRI lumbar spine without contrast — ordered STAT. 2. Cyclobenzaprine 10 mg TID PRN for muscle spasm. 3. Naproxen 500 mg BID with food for pain and inflammation. 4. Strict activity modification — no lifting over 10 lbs, no bending/twisting. Out of work pending MRI results and follow-up. 5. Workers' compensation paperwork initiated — will complete and send to employer. 6. Return precautions reviewed: go to ER immediately if bowel/bladder dysfunction, worsening leg weakness, saddle anesthesia, or inability to ambulate. 7. Follow-up in 1 week or sooner if MRI results return. Patient verbalized understanding.

referrals.to: Radiology — MRI Lumbar Spine without contrast
referrals.reason: Acute lumbar radiculopathy with motor deficit (EHL weakness). Evaluate for disc herniation.

encounters.reasonForVisit: Follow-up for acute lumbar radiculopathy. MRI completed 4/18, results under review.

notes.subjective: Patient returns for follow-up of acute low back injury. Reports pain has improved slightly from 7/10 to 5-6/10 at rest with cyclobenzaprine and naproxen. However, left leg pain persists — still radiating to calf. Tingling in left foot ongoing, especially at night. States the cyclobenzaprine makes him drowsy. Has been staying off work per instructions. Frustrated about being out of work. Asking about timeline for return to duty. MRI was completed 4/18 — patient anxious about results.
notes.objective: Less distress than initial visit. Gait improved but still mildly antalgic. Lumbar paraspinal spasm decreased. Tenderness at L4-L5 persists. SLR still positive on left at 50 degrees (improved from 40). Motor: left EHL 4+/5, unchanged. Sensation: mild decrease to light touch left lateral leg/dorsal foot persists. MRI results reviewed in visit (see imaging results — pending final radiology read at time of encounter documentation).
notes.assessment: 1. Lumbar radiculopathy, left L5, with ongoing motor and sensory deficit. Clinical trajectory improving but incomplete resolution. MRI interpretation pending final radiologist report — preliminary review suggests L4-L5 disc involvement. 2. Work-related injury — continued work restriction appropriate.
notes.plan: 1. Continue cyclobenzaprine 10 mg TID PRN and naproxen 500 mg BID. 2. Await final MRI radiology report — will call patient with results and discuss next steps including possible physical therapy referral vs. spine specialist referral depending on findings. 3. Remain out of work until re-evaluation. Updated workers' comp documentation provided. 4. If symptoms worsen — particularly worsening weakness, new bowel/bladder symptoms — go to ER immediately. 5. Follow-up in 2 weeks or sooner when MRI results finalized. 6. Discussed importance of gentle walking as tolerated. Avoid prolonged sitting or standing.

messages.subject: workers comp form
messages.body: hey i got this workers comp form from my foreman but idk what sections the dr needs to fill out. can i just drop it off at the front desk or do i need an appt for this

thread.body: Hi Marcus, you can drop the form off at the front desk and we'll have the provider complete it. It usually takes 3-5 business days. If you have a follow-up appointment coming up, you can also bring it then and we can go over it with you. Let us know if you have questions.
ok cool ill drop it off tmrw thx

messages.subject: pain getting worse
messages.body: sorry to msg so late but my back is real bad tonite. the leg pain is way worse and my foot keeps going numb. took the cyclobenzaprine and naproxen but its not helping. should i go to the ER?? im kinda freaking out

thread.body: Hi Marcus, thank you for reaching out. I'm sorry you're dealing with increased pain. A few important questions: (1) Are you having any difficulty urinating or having bowel accidents? (2) Do you have numbness in your groin/inner thigh area? (3) Is the weakness in your foot getting worse — can you still pull your toes up toward you? If the answer to any of these is YES, please go to the ER right away. If no to all three, the increased pain is likely a flare of your radiculopathy, which can happen. You can try icing 20 min on / 20 min off. We have your follow-up tomorrow — if you can't wait until then, go to the ER. Call 911 if symptoms suddenly worsen.
no none of that other stuff just the leg and foot. ill try the ice and make it to tmrw. thx for getting back to me

messages.subject: mri results??
messages.body: hey dr kim any word on the mri yet? its been almost a week since i got it done and nobody called me. tryna not to stress about it but u know

thread.body: Hi Marcus, I understand the wait is stressful. The MRI images have come in but the final radiologist report hasn't been finalized yet. I've flagged it as urgent with radiology. As soon as the report is ready, I will call you personally to go over the results and discuss next steps. If we don't have the report by Monday, I'll follow up with radiology again. Hang in there.
